text: Delegate
Oversee
Empower
Accountable
Micromanage
Bottleneck
Outsource
Onboard
Red tape
Bandwidth
Step back
Entrust
I had the clinic accredited last year.
We got the new system rebuilt by an outside firm.
I had my deputy run the morning rounds.
I got my team to take ownership of their cases.
She had her proposal turned down twice before it passed.
So, Eliane. Last time we spoke, you were still signing off on everything yourself. What did you finally let go of?
The billing, first. I had the whole system rebuilt by an outside firm, and I got it outsourced completely.
Good. And the rounds? You were the bottleneck there. Every morning waited for you.
I had my deputy run them without me in the room. It felt wrong for a week. Then it just worked.
That is the hard part -- you stopped trying to micromanage. How did the team react?
Once I got them to take ownership, they rose to it. I made each lead accountable for real outcomes.
And what did all of that buy you?
I had the red tape cut, and it freed up the bandwidth for the science. I finally learned to step back.
Then you have it, Eliane. The clinic runs whether you are there or not. That is leadership done through others.
I had the whole system rebuilt by an outside firm.
I made each lead accountable, and I learned to step back.
The real work of a leader is done through others.

Eliane, it is Oliver. I watched you in the clinic today, and I have to be honest with you as your coach. You are still the bottleneck. You had three people waiting outside your office for a signature that any of them could have given. Here is what I want you to try this month. Pick one system and have it rebuilt by someone else entirely. Take the billing, and get it outsourced. Then take the morning rounds, and have your deputy run them without you in the room. It will feel wrong. You will want to step back in and fix every detail. Do not. Your job now is not to do the work. It is to get good people to own it, and to hold them accountable for the result. Empower them, cut the red tape, and free up your bandwidth for the one thing no one else can do. Call me when you have let something go.

When new residents ask me what leadership is, I tell them what it is not. For years I thought it meant doing everything myself. I had every chart reviewed by me, I signed off on every order, and I was quietly proud of how much I carried. I was also the reason nothing could grow. The hardest lesson of my career was learning to have the work done instead of doing it. I had the systems rebuilt, I got the new doctors onboarded, and I had my deputy run the rounds. I made each lead accountable, and I forced myself to step back and let them decide. It was uncomfortable to entrust a decision to someone who might do it differently. But that is the whole job. The real work of a leader is done through others, and that is the only way anything you build outlasts you.

The real work of a leader is done through others.
